questions: Identify the specific 'UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners' authored by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey, which was last updated in October 2001 and consists of eight distinct tutorials covering topics from file management to UNIX variables. According to the Creative Commons license deed associated with this specific resource, what are the two primary 'Freedoms' granted to the user, and what are the three specific 'Terms' (conditions) that must be met to exercise these freedoms?

rounds.question: Identify the specific 'UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners' authored by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey, which was last updated in October 2001 and consists of eight distinct tutorials covering topics from file management to UNIX variables. According to the Creative Commons license deed associated with this specific resource, what are the two primary 'Freedoms' granted to the user, and what are the three specific 'Terms' (conditions) that must be met to exercise these freedoms?
checklist_width: Freedom 1: Share (copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format)
Freedom 2: Adapt (remix, transform, and build upon the material)
Term 1: Attribution (give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, indicate changes)
Term 2: NonCommercial (may not use the material for commercial purposes)
Term 3: ShareAlike (must distribute contributions under the same license as the original)
checklist_depth: Target Entity: M. Stonebank's UNIX Tutorial for Beginners (University of Surrey)
License Identification: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike (CC BY-NC-SA)
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep Reasoning to identify the specific tutorial based on author, institution, date, and structure (8 tutorials). It then requires Wide Aggregation to retrieve the specific license details (Freedoms and Terms) associated with that resource, which are distinct from generic copyright rules.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
The “UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners” written by M. Stonebank (University of Surrey), last revised October 2001 and comprising eight tutorials, is distributed under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.5 licence.  
Within the licence deed, users are granted two core Freedoms:

1. Share – you are free to copy and redistribute the tutorial in any medium or format.  
2. Adapt – you are free to remix, transform and build upon the material for any purpose except those restricted below. [1][2]

To exercise these freedoms, three binding Terms must be respected:

1. Attribution – you must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the licence and indicate if changes were made. This attribution must not suggest that the original author or the University of Surrey endorses you or your use. [2]  
2. NonCommercial – you may not use the material for commercial purposes; all sharing or adaptations must remain strictly non-commercial. [2]  
3. ShareAlike – if you remix, transform or build upon the tutorial, you must distribute your contributions under the same or a compatible Creative Commons licence, thereby keeping derivative works open on the same terms. [2]

By observing these three conditions—proper attribution, non-commercial use only and share-alike distribution—users may fully exercise the freedoms to copy, share and adapt the eight-part UNIX tutorial while ensuring that future users inherit identical rights.
rounds.answer_b:
The specific resource in question is the "UNIX Tutorial for Beginners," authored by Michael Stonebank (listed as M. Stonebank) at the University of Surrey. This online tutorial series was originally created in 2000 and last updated in October 2001. It comprises eight distinct tutorials designed for novice users, covering foundational UNIX/Linux concepts. These include: Tutorial 1 on listing files and directories; Tutorial 2 on copying, moving, and removing files; Tutorial 3 on displaying file contents; Tutorial 4 on redirection; Tutorial 5 on wildcards and filename conventions; Tutorial 6 on pipes and filters; Tutorial 7 on permissions; and Tutorial 8 on other commands, including UNIX variables such as environment and shell variables [1]. The tutorials emphasize practical file management, command-line operations, and system navigation, making it a widely referenced educational resource for beginners transitioning to UNIX-based systems like Linux.

This resource is associated with a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike (CC BY-NC-SA) 4.0 International License, as indicated in archived versions and mirrored educational sites hosting the content [2]. According to the Creative Commons license deed, users are granted two primary freedoms: (1) Share – the ability to copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format for any purpose, even commercially under certain conditions; and (2) Adapt – the freedom to remix, transform, and build upon the material, allowing modifications or derivative works [3].

To exercise these freedoms, three specific terms (conditions) must be met: (1) Attribution – users must provide appropriate credit to the original author (M. Stonebank and the University of Surrey), include a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made; (2) NonCommercial – the material may not be used for commercial purposes without permission; and (3) ShareAlike – any adapted or remixed versions must be distributed under the identical CC BY-NC-SA license, ensuring derivative works remain open and shareable [3]. These provisions promote open educational access while protecting the author's rights and encouraging collaborative improvements. Failure to comply revokes the freedoms, and no additional restrictions can be imposed beyond the license terms. This licensing framework has enabled the tutorial's widespread dissemination in academic and self-study contexts.

## References
[1] Stonebank, M. (2001). UNIX Tutorial for Beginners. University of Surrey. https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[2] Internet Archive Wayback Machine captures of Surrey UNIX Tutorial (various dates, confirming update and structure). https://web.archive.org/web/*/https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[3] Creative Commons. (n.d.). Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0) License Deed. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A followed the formatting constraints perfectly, using clear lists to separate the 'Freedoms' and 'Terms' as requested. It provided an accurate reflection of the Creative Commons Deed text. Agent B failed significantly in two areas: 1) It included a bizarre internal monologue about word counting at the end of the response (Quality Failure). 2) It contradicted itself in the 'Share' definition, claiming it allows commercial use 'under certain conditions' despite correctly identifying the license as NonCommercial (Logic Failure). Agent A provided a clean, accurate, and user-friendly response.
